title: Can physics-informed neural networks beat the finite element method?
authors: T. G. Grossmann;U. J. Komorowska;J. Latz;C. Schönlieb

paper_summary.problem: Systematically compare PINNs versus the finite element method (FEM) for solving a suite of linear and nonlinear PDEs. The question: can PINNs actually beat FEM in accuracy and runtime?
paper_summary.approach: Solve 1D/2D/3D Poisson, 1D Allen-Cahn, and 1D/2D semilinear Schrödinger with both standard Galerkin FEM (FEniCS) and PINNs (PyTorch). Sweep PINN architectures (layers, nodes) and FEM mesh sizes. Measure relative error on independent test grid, solution-time and evaluation-time.
key_results: For all PDEs tested, FEM achieved same-or-better accuracy at 2–3 orders of magnitude lower cost than PINNs.
PINNs were only competitive on pointwise evaluation cost after training, not on total time to solution.
Larger PINN networks did not yield monotonic accuracy gains; width mattered more than depth.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Carefully-controlled empirical disproof of the common claim that PINNs outperform FEM for standard elliptic/parabolic PDEs.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Analytical/manufactured solutions, generated in-repo

step_by_step: 1. Clone github.com/TamaraGrossmann/FEM-vs-PINNs and install FEniCS + PyTorch.
2. Run 1D Poisson FEM (their fem_poisson1d.py) across mesh refinements; record L2 relative errors vs DOF.
3. Train PINNs at specified architectures on 1D Poisson; record train time and L2 error.
4. Reproduce Pareto plot (Fig 2): FEM dominates PINN in time-accuracy tradeoff.
5. Repeat for Allen-Cahn 1D and confirm qualitative conclusion.
6. Optional: extend with 3D Poisson to reproduce the one regime (eval time) where PINN wins.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match within ~10% on relative-error tables; qualitative reproduction of all Pareto figures.
known_risks: PINN training is stochastic; averaged over several seeds for stable numbers.

followon_questions: Does the PINN-vs-FEM gap close at higher spatial dimensions (d≥5)?
How do adaptive activation functions or Fourier feature embeddings change the Pareto?
Is the conclusion robust under parametric PDE families where FEM must be resolved many times (surrogate regime)?
What happens under irregular geometry where FEM meshing is itself expensive?
Does causal PINN training alter the Allen-Cahn verdict?
rationale_for_priority_change: Perfect match to our needs: open code, FEniCS+PyTorch only, direct quantitative comparison. Keep top rank.

title: Solving High-Dimensional PDEs with Latent Spectral Models
authors: Haixu Wu;Tengge Hu;Huakun Luo;Jianmin Wang;Mingsheng Long

paper_summary.problem: Neural operators work in coordinate space and suffer curse-of-dimensionality for high-dim PDEs. Learn PDEs in a compact latent spectral space.
paper_summary.approach: Latent Spectral Models (LSM) use hierarchical projection to a low-dim latent, then a 'neural spectral block' that approximates the operator with trainable basis functions. Theoretical guarantees via spectral approximation theory.
key_results: SOTA on 7 solid+fluid benchmarks (Elasticity, Plasticity, Navier-Stokes, Darcy, Airfoil, Pipe).
Stable performance across resolutions 32x32 → 1024x1024 on Darcy.
Consistent outperformance of FNO, U-FNO, WMT, Galerkin-Transformer baselines.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Operator learning in a learned latent spectral basis—decoupling representation from output grid.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: PDEBench / Takamoto benchmarks; dataset download links in repo README

reproduction_plan.recommended_compute: 1 GPU (A100 ideal, any modern GPU sufficient)
step_by_step: 1. Clone thuml/Latent-Spectral-Models; install PyTorch per requirements.txt.
2. Download Darcy 2D dataset (small benchmark, fast iteration).
3. Train LSM-S on Darcy (should take ~1–2h on a single GPU); reproduce relative L2 near 0.0065 (paper value).
4. Train FNO baseline from the same repo on same data; compare to LSM.
5. Run resolution test across 64/128/256 on Darcy to replicate resolution-stability figure.
6. (Optional) Navier-Stokes 2D benchmark for a harder check.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match within 5–10% on Darcy & NS-2D L2 errors.

followon_questions: How does LSM perform on 3D turbulence datasets (Johns Hopkins Turbulence Database)?
Can the latent-spectral basis be made physics-aware (e.g., divergence-free)?
What is the sample-efficiency curve vs. FNO?
Does LSM generalize zero-shot across parametric PDE families?
Can the approach be combined with multigrid for hybrid solver acceleration?
rationale_for_priority_change: Well-supported repo and benchmark datasets; strong baselines. Prime candidate.

title: Koopman neural operator as a mesh-free solver of non-linear partial differential equations
authors: Wei Xiong;Xiaomeng Huang;Ziyang Zhang;Ruixuan Deng;Pei Sun;Yang Tian

paper_summary.problem: Existing neural operators become inaccurate and less interpretable for long-term/non-linear PDE evolution.
paper_summary.approach: Koopman Neural Operator (KNO) formulates the PDE solution as a non-linear dynamical system and approximates its Koopman operator (infinite-dim linear) acting on flow embeddings. Uses an FNO-like lifting but with a Koopman block.
key_results: Outperforms FNO on mesh-independent Burgers experiments and long-term prediction on 5 PDEs incl. 2D Navier-Stokes and Rayleigh-Bénard.
Zero-shot prediction across untrained discretization granularities.
Applied to real systems: global water vapor, western boundary currents.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First Koopman-theoretic neural operator with demonstrated long-horizon stability on turbulent PDEs.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Benchmark datasets reused from FNO paper (Burgers, NS-2D) + Copernicus ocean data
specific_numbers_reported: relative-L2 vs FNO tabulated per task

step_by_step: 1. Clone Koopman-Laboratory/KoopmanLab; install via pip install -e .
2. Download FNO's Burgers dataset; run python train_burgers.py with KNO config.
3. Verify reported relative L2 ~ reported value (check paper Table 2).
4. Train FNO baseline for head-to-head.
5. Extend to NS-2D long-term (100+ steps) and check stability.
6. Zero-shot: train at res=32, evaluate at res=64/128.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Within 10-15% on L2; qualitative reproduction of Koopman-eigenvalue analysis.
known_risks: Possible version drift in KoopmanLab package.

followon_questions: How does KNO compare against POD-DL-ROM on same benchmarks?
Is the learned Koopman spectrum interpretable / linked to physical modes?
Can KNO handle boundary changes (mesh-free in a stronger sense)?
Hybrid KNO + classical time-integrator for stability guarantees?
rationale_for_priority_change: Strong code, clear benchmarks. Slightly trickier than LSM due to long-rollout stability.

title: Lifex-cfd: an Open-source Computational Fluid Dynamics Solver for Cardiovascular Applications
authors: P. C. Africa;Ivan Fumagalli;M. Bucelli;Alberto Zingaro;L. Dede’;A. Quarteroni

paper_summary.problem: Open-source CFD solver specialized for cardiovascular simulations (blood flow in moving cardiac chambers + valves).
paper_summary.approach: Finite-element incompressible Navier-Stokes solver on deal.II/PETSc. ALE for moving domains, VMS-LES for transitional turbulence, RIIS for immersed valves. SUPG-PSPG stabilization.
key_results: Verified ideal convergence orders on Beltrami benchmark.
Near-ideal parallel speedup on Taylor-Green vortex.
Patient-specific cases for idealized and real vasculature.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Production-quality, LGPL-v3 cardiovascular CFD with careful verification and parallel scalability.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Zenodo 7852088 - parameter files, meshes, auxiliary data

reproduction_plan.recommended_compute: multi-CPU node; HPC for full cardiac test
step_by_step: 1. Build deal.II with MPI/PETSc/Trilinos; build lifex core, then lifex-cfd.
2. Download Zenodo 7852088; run Beltrami benchmark → verify convergence table.
3. Run Taylor-Green vortex on 32-core node; verify expected kinetic-energy dissipation curve.
4. (Optional) Run one patient-specific idealized aorta case from the provided data.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match on convergence rates and kinetic-energy dissipation curves.
known_risks: Full cardiac cases may require HPC queue time.

followon_questions: Can the lifex-cfd Navier-Stokes solver be coupled with Windkessel BCs from the literature?
Performance vs. OpenFOAM on the same Beltrami benchmark?
Can ML turbulence models replace VMS-LES?
GPU port via deal.II's matrix-free infrastructure?
rationale_for_priority_change: Excellent reproducibility but heavy dependency stack (deal.II + Trilinos) inflates setup time. Great if we want a cardiovascular reference; not a 'start today' pick.

title: Enhancing training of physics-informed neural networks using domain-decomposition based preconditioning strategies

paper_summary.problem: PINN training often converges slowly with L-BFGS; preconditioning is underexplored.
paper_summary.approach: Introduce Schwarz-domain-decomposition-based nonlinear preconditioners (additive SPQN and multiplicative MPQN) that decompose network parameters layer-wise and apply L-BFGS within blocks.
key_results: Significant convergence acceleration vs vanilla L-BFGS/Adam on several PINN benchmarks.
Additive preconditioner is embarrassingly parallel → new model-parallelism route for PINNs.
Lower achieved relative error at fixed training budget.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Brings classical domain-decomposition preconditioning into nonlinear optimization for PINNs.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: Promised upon acceptance (Bitbucket/alena_ referenced in text); SIAM J. Sci. Comput. 2023 version should have code appendix
reproducibility_deep.code_language: python (PyTorch per conventions of Karniadakis group)
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured solutions for Burgers, Allen-Cahn, advection tests
specific_numbers_reported: Erel per optimizer reported in Tables 3-5

step_by_step: 1. Implement layer-wise Schwarz block L-BFGS from scratch guided by Algorithm 1 in paper.
2. Reproduce Burgers PINN baseline with Adam and plain L-BFGS.
3. Add additive SPQN wrapper; verify convergence curves reported in Fig 5.
4. Measure final Erel and compare to paper Tables 3-5.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative reproduction of convergence acceleration; numerical match within 20% given stochasticity.
known_risks: No public code yet → full re-implementation required.
L-BFGS line search finicky in Schwarz setting.

followon_questions: Does the preconditioner help for stiff PINN losses (low-viscosity Burgers)?
How does it compose with causal/self-adaptive PINN weighting?
Theoretical convergence rate of the nonlinear Schwarz iteration.
rationale_for_priority_change: Strong idea, but code not public at time of writing — demotes from A to C until repo is released or full reimplementation budget allotted.

title: Machine learning–accelerated computational fluid dynamics
authors: Dmitrii Kochkov;Jamie A. Smith;Ayya Alieva;Qing Wang;M. Brenner;Stephan Hoyer

paper_summary.problem: DNS of Navier-Stokes is limited by smallest-scale resolution; LES relies on hand-tuned closures.
paper_summary.approach: End-to-end differentiable CFD where a CNN corrects the convective flux on a coarse grid; trained against high-res DNS of 2D Kolmogorov flow with model-consistent rollouts (32 steps).
key_results: Matches DNS accuracy at 8–10× coarser grid → ~40–80× compute speedup.
Stable long-horizon rollouts (tens of thousands of time steps).
Outperforms LES closures at equivalent cost.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First large-scale demonstration of ML-accelerated CFD with DNS-fidelity on 2D turbulence.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Datasets generated in-repo (seeded); also published via TFDS

step_by_step: 1. pip install jax-cfd; run the notebook `notebooks/kolmogorov_flow.ipynb`.
2. Generate ground-truth DNS at 2048² (may need to reduce to 512² for time budget).
3. Train the learned correction model (default config).
4. Compare vorticity correlation vs baseline DNS at same coarse resolution.
5. Reproduce Fig 1 quantitative results: coarse-DNS vs ML-DNS vorticity correlations.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match on vorticity correlation and energy spectra.
known_risks: DNS at 2048² is expensive; may need to cap at 1024².
JAX-cfd's default training can take hours.

followon_questions: Does the same approach work for 3D turbulence?
Hybrid with entropy-stable limiters for shocks?
Training with less high-res data (sample efficiency)?
Integrating this with weather model cores (NWP)?
rationale_for_priority_change: 1123 citations, Google JAX-CFD repo is exemplary, data generation built in. Upgrade to co-#1 priority.

title: Dedalus: A flexible framework for numerical simulations with spectral methods
authors: K. Burns;G. Vasil;J. Oishi;D. Lecoanet;B. Brown

paper_summary.problem: Need a flexible framework to solve general PDEs spectrally in many geometries (Cartesian, spherical, annular).
paper_summary.approach: Dedalus: object-oriented Python framework representing PDEs symbolically; uses modified sparse tau method with Chebyshev/Fourier bases; banded matrices via Dirichlet preconditioning. Parallel via MPI.
key_results: Demonstrated on Kelvin-Helmholtz, Rayleigh-Bénard, Kolmogorov flow, self-gravitating cylinders.
Banded systems give O(N) solves per timestep.
Scalable to O(10k) cores on ALCF/NERSC.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Widely-adopted (485 cites) production-grade spectral PDE framework.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Example scripts in methods_paper_examples repo

step_by_step: 1. conda install -c conda-forge dedalus.
2. Clone DedalusProject/methods_paper_examples.
3. Run 1D Poisson example; verify spectral convergence.
4. Run 2D Kelvin-Helmholtz example; compare to published vorticity field.
5. Run Rayleigh-Bénard at Ra=1e6; check Nusselt number scaling.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Exact match on spectral convergence; qualitative on turbulent cases.
known_risks: Dedalus v3 changed API significantly; methods_paper_examples may target v2.

followon_questions: Benchmark Dedalus vs Julia's ApproxFun + FourierFlows on same problems.
GPU port via JAX or CUDA?
Use for dynamo simulations — replicate Vasil's ICC benchmark.
Coupling with AMR for local resolution.
rationale_for_priority_change: Mature, heavily cited, easy install. Upgrade — excellent starting point.

title: Physics-informed neural networks for solving Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes equations

paper_summary.problem: Turbulence closure is hard; can PINNs learn RANS solutions using only boundary data?
paper_summary.approach: Fully-connected PINN taking (x,y) → (U,V,P,u²,v²,uv). Loss = PDE residual of RANS + data match at boundaries (no interior closure assumption).
key_results: <1% error on laminar Falkner-Skan.
Good accuracy on ZPG/APG boundary layers and NACA-0012 airfoil without a turbulence model.
Reynolds stresses recovered consistently.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Shows PINNs can bypass explicit turbulence closure if boundary Reynolds-stress data are provided.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: None advertised; may be available upon request from authors
reproducibility_deep.data_source: DNS/LES reference data for ZPG/APG BL and NACA (public DNS databases)
specific_numbers_reported: <1% error on laminar case (quoted in abstract)

step_by_step: 1. Set up a 5-output PINN in DeepXDE with RANS residual.
2. Generate Falkner-Skan analytical solution; train; expect <1% error.
3. Use Jiménez DNS for ZPG BL as boundary data; train.
4. Compare predicted U,V,P,u²,v²,uv against DNS at interior stations.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative (shapes of U,V profiles); quantitative L2 within ~10% of paper.
known_risks: PINN for turbulent cases is notoriously sensitive to loss-weight scheduling.

followon_questions: Does it extend to 3D separated flows?
Comparison against data-driven SA or k-omega closures?
Uncertainty quantification on the recovered Reynolds stresses.
Integration with real PIV data instead of DNS.
Does causal/time-marching PINN improve on this steady formulation?
rationale_for_priority_change: Clear, impactful paper but no public code lowers feasibility; still a B-tier candidate given DeepXDE reimplementation is tractable.

title: Learning in Modal Space: Solving Time-Dependent Stochastic PDEs Using Physics-Informed Neural Networks

paper_summary.problem: Long-time integration of stochastic PDEs with arbitrary initial data — classical DO/BO methods have singular covariance and eigenvalue-crossing issues.
paper_summary.approach: NN-DO and NN-BO: parameterize modes and coefficients by neural networks; enforce DO/BO constraints implicitly in the loss (no explicit time derivatives of modes).
key_results: Works when original DO fails (deterministic IC case).
RMSE for BO random coefficients ~0.05–0.29 at early/late times.
Handles long-time integration via subdomain-in-time decomposition.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Trainable DO/BO decomposition avoiding the pathological assumptions of classical versions.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: None advertised; possibly available from Karniadakis group on request
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured SPDE problems (linear stochastic advection, stochastic diffusion-reaction, etc.)

step_by_step: 1. Reimplement the DO/BO loss with NN parameterization (algorithms 1-2 in paper).
2. Benchmark on stochastic advection; match RMSE ~0.05 after 1000 iterations.
3. Extend to diffusion-reaction; match Table 5 random-coefficient RMSE.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative match; quantitative within 2× of reported RMSE.

followon_questions: Comparison to stochastic Galerkin + PC expansion.
How much training data does the inverse setup require?
rationale_for_priority_change: Niche but no public code; demote to C.

title: Poisson Flow Generative Models

paper_summary.problem: Normalizing flows / SDE-based generative models have slow sampling and mode-collapse issues.
paper_summary.approach: Interpret data as charges in z=0 plane of a (d+1)-dim space; the gradient of the Poisson equation solution induces a deterministic flow that maps data → uniform hemisphere. Backward ODE gives sampling.
key_results: Inception 9.68, FID 2.35 on CIFAR-10 (SOTA at time among normalizing flows).
10-20× faster sampling than VP/VE-SDE baselines at equal FID.
Robust to network choice and step size.
paper_summary.main_contribution: A PDE-physics-inspired generative model with fewer sampling steps and good FID.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: CIFAR-10, CelebA (public)

step_by_step: 1. Clone Newbeeer/poisson_flow.
2. Use provided CIFAR-10 config; train.
3. Evaluate FID/IS using their eval script.
4. Reproduce sampling-speedup table (Table in paper).
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: FID within ~0.2 of published 2.35.
known_risks: FID calculation depends on reference stats; ensure matching CIFAR-10 stats file.

followon_questions: How does PFGM compare to later PFGM++ and diffusion-transformer models?
Does the Poisson analogy extend to discrete data (text)?
Connection to score matching and Wasserstein flows.
Ablate the hemisphere geometry — is it necessary?
rationale_for_priority_change: Not really a PDE-solver paper (it's a generative model using Poisson as analogy). Good code but off-topic for PDE replication; B tier at best for 'PDE' bucket.

title: LNO: Laplace Neural Operator for Solving Differential Equations

paper_summary.problem: FNO handles only periodic signals and cannot represent non-periodic transient responses well.
paper_summary.approach: Laplace Neural Operator (LNO): replace Fourier transform block with Laplace transform (pole-residue representation). Natural handling of transients via poles.
key_results: LNO with 1 layer beats FNO with 4 layers on 3 ODEs (Duffing, driven pendulum, Lorenz) and 3 PDEs (Euler-Bernoulli beam, diffusion, reaction-diffusion).
Exponential convergence claimed for linear problems.
Better transient-response capture in undamped cases.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Laplace-transform-based neural operator handling non-periodic and transient signals.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Generated in-repo via SciPy ODE solvers and analytical solutions
specific_numbers_reported: Relative L2 per benchmark (Table 2 of paper)

step_by_step: 1. Clone qianyingcao/Laplace-Neural-Operator.
2. Run Duffing training; verify relative L2 < FNO baseline.
3. Run reaction-diffusion; verify Table 2 values.
4. Sweep # poles; produce the convergence-vs-poles plot.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match on Table 2 within 10%.
known_risks: Complex-valued arithmetic in pole-residue layer can have numerical issues.

followon_questions: Extension to 2D PDEs on non-periodic domains?
Can the learned poles be interpreted physically?
Efficiency vs. FNO at equal parameter count.
rationale_for_priority_change: Clean scope, public code, lightweight benchmarks. Solid A pick.

title: Walk on Stars: A Grid-Free Monte Carlo Method for PDEs with Neumann Boundary Conditions

paper_summary.problem: Walk-on-spheres Monte Carlo solves only Dirichlet Laplace problems; no Neumann BCs.
paper_summary.approach: Walk on Stars (WoSt) samples on star-shaped regions whose boundaries include Neumann-boundary arcs; sampling via visibility silhouette. Solves linear elliptic PDEs with mixed BCs without meshing.
key_results: First grid-free MC method for Neumann conditions.
Works on arbitrary geometries (non-convex) and extreme geometric complexity.
Progressive output (like ray tracing) → useful for graphics.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Fundamentally extends WoS to the full mixed-BC regime with practical BVH-based implementation.

reproducibility_deep.code_language: C++ (with Python bindings); also simple Python reference
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Example scenes in repo

step_by_step: 1. Clone wost-simple; build (CMake + C++17).
2. Run 2D Laplace demo with mixed Dirichlet/Neumann.
3. Verify pointwise convergence rate N^{-1/2}.
4. Compare against FEniCS reference solution on same geometry.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative MC convergence rate match; pointwise values within 1σ of reference.

followon_questions: Extension to Robin BCs and anisotropic media?
Variance reduction via control variates with WoS.
Walk on Stars for PDE-constrained optimization.
rationale_for_priority_change: Beautiful paper, great code. A-tier.

title: Fast Poisson solvers for spectral methods

paper_summary.problem: Fast Poisson solvers exist for FD/FE but not for spectral methods; spectral accuracy traditionally couples with O(n^3) cost.
paper_summary.approach: Ultraspherical spectral discretization → Sylvester matrix equation with 'separated spectra' → ADI method gives O(n^2 log^2 n) complexity. Works on square, cylinder, solid sphere, cube.
key_results: 100M DOF Poisson on a square solved in < 2 minutes on laptop.
Spectral accuracy with quasi-optimal complexity.
Integrated into Chebfun via chebfun2.poisson.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First optimal-complexity spectral Poisson solver via ADI on Sylvester equations.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Analytical test functions in repo
specific_numbers_reported: accuracy to machine precision for analytic RHS

step_by_step: 1. Install Chebfun in MATLAB/Octave.
2. u = chebfun2.poisson(f, BC, n) for n=4096 → measure time.
3. Verify runtime scaling across n=64,...,16384.
4. Benchmark vs. MATLAB's built-in Poisson solver on same grid.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Exact match on runtime scaling; residual at machine precision.
known_risks: MATLAB license; Octave support for Chebfun is partial.

followon_questions: Biharmonic and Helmholtz analogs via same ADI approach?
Use as preconditioner in more general elliptic solvers.
rationale_for_priority_change: Classic, clean, reproducible. MATLAB/Chebfun dependency is minor. A-tier.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Mesh Refinement

paper_summary.problem: AMR refinement heuristics are ad-hoc; can RL learn better refinement policies?
paper_summary.approach: Formulate AMR as POMDP with local per-element observation. Train deep Q-network to refine/coarsen HDG-FEM elements on advection and Poisson.
key_results: RL agent reduces DOF by ~20-30% at fixed error vs heuristic.
Generalizes across PDEs (trained on advection, tested on Poisson).
Demonstrated on 1D & 2D.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First practical RL-AMR controller shown to beat bulk-refinement heuristic on DG-FEM.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured solutions

reproduction_plan.recommended_compute: 1 GPU for RL training + CPU for DG
step_by_step: 1. Build advection HDG solver in MFEM with refinement API.
2. Wrap as Gym environment: obs=element residual features, action=refine/coarsen/nothing.
3. Train DQN per paper Section 4.
4. Compare agent vs bulk(theta=0.5) heuristic on Pareto.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative reproduction of Pareto dominance; quantitative within 30%.

followon_questions: Can the agent generalize across solver types (FVM, DG, CG)?
Multi-agent RL for parallel AMR load-balancing.
Use of transformer-based policy for long-range correlations.
RL-AMR for time-dependent problems with shocks.
Offline RL from existing AMR simulation data.
rationale_for_priority_change: Dead code repo. Reimplementation cost is high for what it delivers.

title: Godunov loss functions for modelling of hyperbolic conservation laws

paper_summary.problem: Standard PINNs fail for hyperbolic conservation laws with shocks because PDE residual is ill-defined at discontinuities.
paper_summary.approach: Replace PDE-residual loss with finite-volume-based Godunov/approximate-Riemann-solver flux loss — 'Godunov loss'. Evaluate losses as cell-averaged flux imbalances.
key_results: Beats standard PINN and FVM-based (no-Riemann) losses on 2D Riemann problem.
Successful time-stepping and super-resolution reconstruction.
Tested across 6 classes of Riemann problems (19 admissible initial conditions).
paper_summary.main_contribution: Entropy-consistent shock-capturing loss function for neural PDE solvers.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: Check CMAME supplementary; search R. Cassia or Kerswell group pages
reproducibility_deep.data_source: 2D Riemann initial conditions (analytical)

step_by_step: 1. Reimplement Godunov loss with HLLC or Roe flux.
2. Generate 2D Riemann initial conditions for one representative configuration per class.
3. Train PINN with Godunov loss; compare to standard PDE loss.
4. Verify sharpness of captured shocks vs TV.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative reproduction of shock sharpness; quantitative MSE within 2×.

followon_questions: Does WENO loss do better than Godunov loss?
rationale_for_priority_change: Important topic, tractable reimplementation, but no public code. B tier.

title: Plane Wave Discontinuous Galerkin Methods for the 2D Helmholtz Equation: Analysis of the p-Version

paper_summary.problem: Plane-wave DG methods (Trefftz type) for Helmholtz equation lacked sharp p-version convergence theory.
paper_summary.approach: Prove p-version convergence estimates for plane-wave DG on 2D Helmholtz; builds on ultra-weak variational formulation.
key_results: Algebraic convergence rate in p with explicit constants.
Connection to standard p-FEM theory.
Numerical experiments confirming theoretical rates.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Rigorous p-version analysis of plane-wave DG for Helmholtz.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: Not advertised; Hiptmair group ETH may have MATLAB code.
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Analytical Helmholtz solutions on polygonal domains
benchmark_problems: 2D Helmholtz on square with Robin BCs

step_by_step: 1. Implement plane-wave DG basis in NGSolve.
2. Solve 2D Helmholtz on square for k=1,5,10; vary p.
3. Measure L2 error vs analytical solution; plot convergence in p.
4. Compare observed rate to theory.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match on p-convergence rate.
known_risks: Plane-wave basis has conditioning issues for large p.

rationale_for_priority_change: Theory-heavy, no code, specialized niche. Demote.

title: Galerkin Approximations for the Stochastic Burgers Equation

paper_summary.problem: Rigorous L∞ convergence rates for Galerkin approximations of stochastic Burgers & related SPDEs.
paper_summary.approach: Prove spectral Galerkin approximations converge in L∞ (not just L2) for semilinear stochastic evolution equations with additive noise. Apply to stochastic heat, reaction-diffusion, Burgers.
key_results: Explicit rate N^{-γ} in truncation with γ tied to noise regularity.
L∞ error uniformly in space (new — previously only L2 known).
Numerical experiments illustrating rates.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First L∞ error estimates for Galerkin-SPDE approximations.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured SPDE solutions

step_by_step: 1. Implement Fourier Galerkin solver for stochastic Burgers.
2. Use Gaussian white-noise Q-Wiener forcing.
3. Monte Carlo over 100+ paths; measure L∞ error.
4. Verify empirical rate matches theoretical γ.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative rate match.
known_risks: Monte Carlo variance; need many paths.

followon_questions: L∞ rates for finite-element vs spectral.
rationale_for_priority_change: Pure-math paper; easy to reimplement numerics but limited 'reproduction' since no code. C tier.

title: FLUPS: A Fourier-Based Library of Unbounded Poisson Solvers

paper_summary.problem: Fast Poisson solvers needed for simulations with mixed boundary types (periodic, Neumann, unbounded), particularly vortex methods.
paper_summary.approach: FLUPS: MPI+OpenMP C++ library, FFT-based Green's function convolution, spectral to order 10; supports all 3x3x3=27 BC combinations. Optimized all-to-all and point-to-point communications.
key_results: Convergence orders 2 to 10+ achieved.
Strong scaling to 32K cores on unbounded Poisson.
Faster than PMG/FMM at same accuracy for cell-centered unbounded problems.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Production-grade MPI Poisson library handling the full BC matrix.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Analytical Poisson test problems in repo

reproduction_plan.recommended_compute: multi-CPU node; HPC for scaling test
step_by_step: 1. Clone flups; build with FFTW.
2. Run convergence test on 3D Poisson; verify order 2,4,6,8,10.
3. Strong-scaling test on 128-1024 cores.
4. Compare timing with PETSc's FFT Poisson.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Exact match on convergence orders; qualitative match on scaling.
known_risks: Access to HPC queue for scaling study.

followon_questions: Integration with a vortex particle method.
rationale_for_priority_change: Strong code but HPC-oriented; needs multi-node to really show off. B tier.

title: Kernel‐based active subspaces with application to computational fluid dynamics parametric problems using the discontinuous Galerkin method

paper_summary.problem: Active subspaces reduce parameter space linearly; most engineering problems have nonlinear active structures.
paper_summary.approach: Kernel-based Active Subspaces (KAS) generalize AS with feature maps (Random Fourier Features, radial kernels) and multivariate output objectives. Pipeline with DG-FEM CFD.
key_results: KAS outperforms linear AS on test functions with nonlinear ridge structure.
Applied to DG-FEM CFD parametric problem (airfoil).
Response-surface accuracy better than Gaussian process baselines.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Kernel-AS method for nonlinear dimension reduction in PDE-parametric problems.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Benchmark test functions + HopeFOAM CFD runs
specific_numbers_reported: R² of response surface across test functions

step_by_step: 1. Install ATHENA from mathLab.
2. Run radial test function experiments; reproduce linear-vs-kernel AS comparison.
3. (Optional) Run HopeFOAM airfoil case for CFD pipeline.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative R² match on test functions.
known_risks: HopeFOAM case requires DG solver stack.

followon_questions: Use in Bayesian optimization for expensive PDE surrogates.
rationale_for_priority_change: Tool-chain heavier than advertised; fine B-tier candidate for UQ/surrogates.

title: On the convergence of discontinuous Galerkin/Hermite spectral methods for the Vlasov-Poisson system

paper_summary.problem: DG/Hermite-spectral methods for Vlasov-Poisson lacked a priori convergence theory.
paper_summary.approach: Use asymmetrically weighted Hermite in velocity + DG in space. Introduce time-dependent weighted L2 space. Prove stability + error estimate.
key_results: Global weighted L2 stability.
Propagation of regularity.
Error estimate in weighted L2.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First convergence theory for DG/Hermite Vlasov-Poisson.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured Vlasov-Poisson solutions

step_by_step: 1. Implement DG-Hermite Vlasov-Poisson from scratch in Python + FEniCSx.
2. Landau damping test; verify damping rate matches linear theory.
3. Refine Hermite moments; measure convergence.
4. Compare to reported rate.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative match; quantitative rate within 20%.
known_risks: Significant implementation effort; weighted-Hermite basis conditioning.

rationale_for_priority_change: Theory paper; heavy reimplementation, no code. C tier.

title: Improvements to the APBS biomolecular solvation software suite
authors: E. Jurrus;David Engel;Keith T. Star;Kyle Monson;Juan Brandi;Lisa E. Felberg;David H. Brookes;Leighton Wilson;Jiahui Chen;Karina Liles;Minju Chun;Peter Li;D. Gohara;Todd J. Dolinsky;R. Konečný;D. Koes;

paper_summary.problem: Update and extend APBS for biomolecular electrostatics (Poisson-Boltzmann equation).
paper_summary.approach: New features in APBS suite: analytical/semi-analytical PBE solver, boundary element solver, geometric flow solvation, graph-based pKa, visualization.
key_results: Scalable to large biomolecules.
Multiple solver backends (FD multigrid, BEM, analytical).
Validated on standard benchmark sets.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Reference biomolecular electrostatics toolkit, widely used (2081 cites).

reproducibility_deep.data_source: PQR files from PDB, provided in docs
specific_numbers_reported: Solvation energies to 1-2% vs finer grids

step_by_step: 1. conda install -c conda-forge apbs pdb2pqr.
2. Run example inputs from APBS tutorial (1ake lysozyme etc).
3. Reproduce solvation energy values.
4. Use geometric-flow solver on one test; compare to FD-multigrid.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match to within 1-2%.
known_risks: Compiling APBS from source is painful; conda package is stable.

followon_questions: Coupling with MD via AMBER PBSA.
rationale_for_priority_change: Mature code, easy conda install. Upgrade — a very safe starter replication.

title: Fast Nonsymmetric Iterations and Preconditioning for Navier-Stokes Equations

paper_summary.problem: Classic Navier-Stokes linear systems are non-symmetric + indefinite; few preconditioners give mesh-independent convergence.
paper_summary.approach: Block preconditioners for discrete Oseen operator: diagonal with Schur-complement approximations. Prove eigenvalues bounded independent of h.
key_results: GMRES iterations independent of mesh size h.
Inner iterative solvers for preconditioner keep costs low.
Validated on backward-facing step and lid-driven cavity.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Foundational paper (258 cites) for block preconditioning of incompressible Navier-Stokes — precursor to PCD/LSC.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: None advertised (1996 paper); modern equivalents in IFISS (MATLAB)
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Standard Stokes/Oseen benchmarks
specific_numbers_reported: GMRES iterations vs h in Tables 2-4

step_by_step: 1. Download IFISS; run navier_testproblem with the block preconditioner.
2. Reproduce iteration-vs-h table at multiple Re.
3. Verify mesh-independence claim.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative iteration-count reproduction.
known_risks: Original 1996 numbers may differ slightly from IFISS defaults.

followon_questions: Compare to LSC and PCD preconditioners.
rationale_for_priority_change: Classic, reproducible via IFISS. Solid B.

title: New Laplace and Helmholtz solvers

paper_summary.problem: Standard FEM/BEM methods have O(h^p) polynomial convergence on polygons with corners.
paper_summary.approach: Rational-function approximations (lightning solvers) with exponential clustering of poles near corners give fractional-exponential (root-exponential) convergence for 2D Laplace/Helmholtz.
key_results: e^{-c√N} convergence for 2D Laplace on polygons with corners.
Orders of magnitude faster than FEM/BEM for comparable accuracy.
Implemented in lightning.m (MATLAB).
paper_summary.main_contribution: Paradigm shift toward rational-function PDE solvers (lightning Laplace solver).

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Analytical Laplace problems on L-shape, polygons
specific_numbers_reported: Root-exponential rate e^{-c√N} confirmed to 10-digit accuracy

step_by_step: 1. Download lightning.m from Trefethen's page.
2. Run L-shape example; verify 10-digit accuracy.
3. Vary N; plot convergence vs e^{-c√N}.
4. Compare time-to-10-digits vs FEniCS on same geometry.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Exact match at machine precision.

followon_questions: Python port (already exists via Baddoo's lightning-python)?
rationale_for_priority_change: Short, clean, high-impact paper with a one-file MATLAB demo. Perfect reproduction target.

title: Stability and error analysis of a class of high-order IMEX schemes for Navier-stokes equations with periodic boundary conditions

paper_summary.problem: High-order IMEX schemes for Navier-Stokes lacked unconditional-stability error analysis.
paper_summary.approach: Scalar-auxiliary-variable (SAV) technique + IMEX k-step (k=1..5) + Fourier-Galerkin space. Prove unconditional L∞-H^1 stability and error estimates.
key_results: First unconditionally stable high-order IMEX for Navier-Stokes.
Up to 5th-order in time, confirmed numerically.
Successful on double-shear-layer problem.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Rigorous high-order IMEX-SAV framework for incompressible NS.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: Shen group sometimes publishes MATLAB on personal pages — check
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured solutions + double shear layer IC
specific_numbers_reported: Orders 1-5 in time verified (Table 5.1 of paper)

step_by_step: 1. Implement Fourier pseudo-spectral Navier-Stokes in 2D.
2. Add SAV auxiliary variable; implement IMEX-BDFk for k=1..5.
3. Manufactured-solution test; verify order k in Δt.
4. Double shear layer at Re=10000; compare vorticity to reported figure.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative order match; qualitative vorticity match.

rationale_for_priority_change: Clear numerics but no code; reimplementation tractable.

title: Rate optimality of adaptive finite element methods with respect to the overall computational costs

paper_summary.problem: Adaptive FEM rate-optimality theory typically measured vs # DOF, not vs computational cost.
paper_summary.approach: Analyze adaptive FEM with inexact iterative solvers (CG for linear, Zarantonello for nonlinear monotone). Prove quasi-optimal linear convergence w.r.t. total cost.
key_results: Rate-optimality in overall cost (not just DOF).
Applies to linear elliptic and nonlinear monotone problems.
Numerical experiments confirm.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First rate-optimality theorem w.r.t. overall computational cost for inexact AFEM.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: TU Wien group sometimes publishes MATLAB
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Standard elliptic test problems

step_by_step: 1. Implement residual-based AFEM in FEniCS with Dörfler marking.
2. Use inexact CG for linear system; adjust solver tolerance adaptively.
3. Track error vs cumulative solver cost on L-shape.
4. Confirm algebraic rate -1 w.r.t. cost.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative reproduction of rate.

rationale_for_priority_change: Theory-focused, no code. C.

title: Comparison of Adaptive Multiresolution and Adaptive Mesh Refinement Applied to Simulations of the Compressible Euler Equations
authors: R. Deiterding;M. O. Domingues;S. Gomes;K. Schneider

paper_summary.problem: AMR and adaptive-multiresolution (MR) are two dominant approaches for PDEs with sharp fronts; which is better?
paper_summary.approach: Head-to-head FV comparison on compressible Euler (2D Riemann Lax-Liu #6, 3D expanding shock). Both use 2nd-order shock-capturing + local time-stepping.
key_results: MR has better memory compression and slightly enhanced convergence.
AMR has lower absolute overhead in tested codes.
CPU-time compression trends similar for both.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Quantitative comparison settling MR vs AMR tradeoffs on standard Euler benchmarks.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: https://github.com/waveletApplications/carmen (HTTP 200) for MR side; AMROC for AMR
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Riemann IC analytical

step_by_step: 1. Build Carmen; run Lax-Liu #6.
2. Build AMROC; run same problem.
3. Compare memory and CPU vs regular-grid baseline.
4. Confirm MR memory advantage.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative compression factor match within 20%.

rationale_for_priority_change: Both codes exist but heavy-weight; mid-tier B.

title: Kinetic.jl: A portable finite volume toolbox for scientific and neural computing

paper_summary.problem: Need a flexible FV framework for kinetic equations + scientific ML in Julia.
paper_summary.approach: Kinetic.jl: modular FV toolbox with KitBase (physics) and KitML (neural closures). Supports multi-scale Boltzmann-Navier-Stokes transitions.
key_results: Solves Navier-Stokes and Boltzmann in unified API.
Neural closure example for BGK collision operator.
Short JOSS paper (software publication).
paper_summary.main_contribution: Unified kinetic-theory + ML Julia toolbox.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Test scripts in repo

step_by_step: 1. Install Julia 1.10+; add Kinetic.jl.
2. Run examples/sod.jl; verify shock tube matches analytical Riemann solution.
3. Run Couette flow.
4. Reproduce a neural-closure example from KitML.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative match on shock positions/states.
known_risks: Julia version compatibility with older package commits.

followon_questions: Multi-scale coupling between kinetic and NS regimes.
rationale_for_priority_change: Julia ease + short paper = very low friction A-tier replication.

title: Highly efficient and energy dissipative schemes for the time fractional Allen-Cahn equation

paper_summary.problem: Time-fractional Allen-Cahn equation needs stable schemes on graded meshes for efficient long-time simulation.
paper_summary.approach: Split fractional derivative into local + history. Use L1/L1-CN/L1+-CN for local; extended SAV for nonlinearity + history. Fourier-Galerkin space.
key_results: Unconditional energy dissipation proven.
Dissipation holds on graded mesh.
Numerical confirmation on 2D Allen-Cahn.
paper_summary.main_contribution: First unconditionally dissipative high-order scheme for time-fractional Allen-Cahn.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Manufactured + standard Allen-Cahn IC

step_by_step: 1. Implement L1+-CN on graded mesh with sum-of-exponentials history.
2. Manufactured solution test for convergence order.
3. 2D Allen-Cahn coarsening; verify energy monotonicity.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative order match.

rationale_for_priority_change: No code; specialized.

title: Modified Poisson-Nernst-Planck Model with Coulomb and Hard-sphere Correlations

paper_summary.problem: Standard Poisson-Nernst-Planck misses Coulomb and hard-sphere correlations important for multivalent ions at interfaces.
paper_summary.approach: Add Coulomb correlation via generalized Debye-Hückel (self-Green's function) and hard-sphere correlation via modified fundamental measure theory. Solve via WKB asymptotics for parallel-plate geometry.
key_results: Reproduces charge-inversion effects seen in Monte Carlo simulations.
Agreement with MC for multivalent ions.
Analytical WKB solution for two-plate case.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Modified PNP with both long- and short-range corrections beyond mean-field.

reproducibility_deep.data_source: Reference MC data from cited papers

step_by_step: 1. Implement generalized Debye-Hückel equation solver (1D).
2. Add MFMT hard-sphere correction.
3. WKB asymptotic solution for two plates.
4. Compare density profiles to reference MC data.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Qualitative match on charge inversion.

followon_questions: Benchmark vs classical DFT of ions.
rationale_for_priority_change: Niche physics; no code.

title: Optimized Schwarz Methods without Overlap for the Helmholtz Equation

paper_summary.problem: Classical Schwarz for Helmholtz fails without overlap (propagating modes undamped).
paper_summary.approach: Derive optimized Robin-type transmission conditions (symbol σ(k) optimized in Fourier). Proves convergence without overlap for Helmholtz.
key_results: Optimal transmission condition gives convergence in finite steps (nonlocal) + local approximation.
Dramatic iteration-count reduction over classical Schwarz.
Numerical validation on 2D Helmholtz benchmark.
paper_summary.main_contribution: Foundational (403 cites) optimized Schwarz theory for Helmholtz.

reproducibility_deep.code_url: None advertised (2002 paper); modern HPDDM library includes OSM
reproducibility_deep.data_source: Analytical Helmholtz solutions
specific_numbers_reported: Iteration counts for classical vs optimized Schwarz

step_by_step: 1. Implement Schwarz iteration with classical vs optimized Robin BC in FreeFem++.
2. 2D Helmholtz on [0,1]² at k=10.
3. Compare iteration counts.
4. Reproduce convergence-rate curves from paper.
reproduction_plan.expected_fidelity: Quantitative iteration-count reproduction.

rationale_for_priority_change: Classic paper reproducible via HPDDM or direct FreeFem++ implementation.
